summary: Human prenatal development spans approximately 38 weeks (266 days) from fertilisation to delivery.
Human prenatal development spans approximately 52 weeks from fertilisation to delivery.
Human prenatal development spans approximately 30 weeks from fertilisation to delivery.
The first two weeks are the all-or-none period: teratogens either kill the embryo or cause no malformation.
The first two weeks are the most sensitive period for organ malformations.
The first two weeks are called the foetal period when growth predominates.
Ontogeny refers to the developmental history of an individual, while phylogeny refers to the evolutionary history of a species.
Phylogeny refers to the development of an individual, while ontogeny refers to evolution of a species.
Both ontogeny and phylogeny refer to individual development.
The embryonic period (weeks 3-8) is the most susceptible to teratogens because organogenesis occurs during this time.
The foetal period (weeks 9-38) is the most susceptible to teratogens because the foetus is larger.
The pre-implantation period (days 1-6) is the most susceptible to teratogens.
The three primary germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) are formed during gastrulation in the third week.
The three germ layers are formed during cleavage in the first week.
The three germ layers are formed during the foetal period.
The zygote undergoes cleavage to form a morula, which develops a cavity to become a blastocyst.
The zygote directly forms a blastocyst without going through the morula stage.
The morula is formed after the blastocyst stage.
